Correctly handled negative number sign flips.

completed.thinking_breakthrough: Only the minimum historical value influences every future decision.

history.notes: Successful R1 active recall completed in 5 minutes. The learner recalled why unlimited transactions remove the minPrice state, derived the positive-adjacent-difference rule, explained the loop boundary required by prices[i + 1], wrote the Java implementation from memory, and passed a dry run with O(n) time and O(1) space.

completed.thinking_breakthrough: Discovered that a good index is one that can eventually reach another good index. Realized that because jump lengths represent a continuous reachable range, only the leftmost good index needs to be tracked.
completed.main_mistake: Initially focused on direct reachability and trying jump combinations. Also assumed every discovered good index had to be preserved before proving that the leftmost good index subsumes all later good indices.

completed.thinking_breakthrough: Derived the greedy frontier expansion algorithm from first principles. Realized the algorithm does not choose the next landing index. Instead, it repeatedly discovers the maximum reachable boundary unlocked by one additional jump by exploring every index in the current reachable region.
completed.main_mistake: Initially reasoned in terms of jumping to individual indices instead of expanding reachable regions. Also attempted to redesign initialization without first validating the loop invariant and boundary conditions.

completed.thinking_breakthrough: Realized tankBalance is candidate-relative state: it represents net fuel accumulated from the current startCandidate to the current scan index, not fuel accumulated from index 0.
completed.main_mistake: Initially treated tankBalance as global fuel, expected the scan to restart after failure, and assumed changing startCandidate meant changing the loop variable.

history.notes: Successful R1 active recall. The learner reconstructed the complete solution from first principles, re-derived the candidate-elimination proof and global feasibility condition, identified the required implementation state before coding, wrote the implementation from memory, and passed a dry run. The session also covered temporary startCandidate == n and the optional single-totalBalance optimization.

implementation_discoveries: scan pointer and candidate pointer are different concepts
tankBalance belongs to the current hypothesis
resetting tankBalance is changing state semantics, not discarding information
final decision depends on global totals rather than local tankBalance

variable_semantics.startCandidate: Current hypothesis for the starting station.
variable_semantics.tankBalance: Accumulated net fuel from startCandidate to the current scan index.
variable_semantics.totalGasAccumulation: Global available fuel across all stations.
variable_semantics.totalGasConsumption: Global required fuel across all travel edges.

questions: Why is startCandidate only a hypothesis?
Why can failed candidates be discarded?
Why are global totals maintained separately?

completed.thinking_breakthrough: Realized Candy requires satisfying two independent adjacent constraints with directional passes: left-to-right handles the left neighbor, right-to-left handles the right neighbor, and the minimum valid final assignment takes the maximum requirement at each index.
completed.main_mistake: Initially considered satisfying one directional neighbor constraint, but identified that one-pass greedy decisions are insufficient because the opposite neighbor constraint can force a larger assignment.

session_summary.solution: Standard O(n) time and O(n) extra-space two-pass greedy solution fully understood.
session_summary.deferred_optimization: The O(1) extra-space optimization exists but was intentionally not studied or derived in this session.

conceptual_discoveries: one directional neighbor constraint is insufficient
left-to-right pass satisfies the left-neighbor constraint
right-to-left pass satisfies the right-neighbor constraint
both adjacent constraints must hold simultaneously
max-merging directional assignments preserves both constraints
max-merging produces the minimum valid standard assignment
implementation_discoveries: each directional pass has one responsibility
the final candy count for each child is the maximum of the two directional requirements
standard implementation uses O(n) extra space for directional assignment state

revision_material.focus: Verify the standard O(n) time and O(n) extra-space two-pass greedy construction.
questions: Why does a one-pass left-to-right solution fail?
What constraint does the left-to-right pass satisfy?
What constraint does the right-to-left pass satisfy?
Why do we take the maximum of the two directional assignments?
Why is the max-merged assignment minimum while satisfying both constraints?
What is the standard time and space complexity?
revision_material.deferred_scope: Do not require the O(1) extra-space optimization for normal revision pass/fail until the open deferred learning is naturally resolved.

completed.thinking_breakthrough: Reframed pair-search into missing-partner search: for each element, compute requiredPartner = target - currentElement and replace repeated linear searches with constant-time lookup.
completed.main_mistake: Initially focused on searching for the partner rather than identifying the repeated query that should drive the data-structure choice.

completed.thinking_breakthrough: Derived Contains Duplicate from the repeated query: Have I already seen this value? This led directly to the required state of previously processed values and the minimal HashSet structure.
completed.main_mistake: No implementation mistake recorded. The key trap analyzed was that inserting before checking would violate the processed-prefix invariant and allow the current element to match itself.

thinking_profile.notes: Student shows strong algorithm reasoning, good invariant recognition, strong state-design intuition, and deep concept learning instead of memorization. OBS-001 R2 showed improved state interpretation and proof discipline. OBS-002 R1 clarified that the main current gap is Implementation Engineering: the learner can derive the correct algorithm but needs to derive initialization, loop boundaries, update ordering, global answer ownership, and return value from the state definition before coding. OBS-007 strengthened greedy reasoning, candidate-relative state modelling, observation-vs-hypothesis separation, and proof-to-implementation mapping. OBS-002 R2 showed the previous implementation gap did not recur: the learner reproduced the algorithm and implementation from memory with no hints and no mistakes. OBS-008 confirmed mastery of the standard O(n) time and O(n) extra-space Candy solution; the O(1) space optimization is intentionally deferred as an open learning item, not a weakness or failure. CPX-001 strengthened lookup-state construction: the learner derived value-to-index storage from the repeated missing-partner query and justified search-before-insertion. CPX-002 reinforced the same lookup-state construction with a smaller state than CPX-001: membership only, so HashSet instead of HashMap.

history.summary: R2 successful active recall. Initialization misconception corrected; no remaining conceptual gaps recorded.

OBS-007 completed. Major breakthrough: tankBalance is candidate-relative state, not global fuel. Implementation breakthrough: scan pointer and startCandidate are separate; candidate changes do not restart the scan. Interview breakthrough: local greedy elimination must be paired with global feasibility. Key invariant: negative tankBalance eliminates every candidate in the failed segment. Reusable pattern: greedy hypothesis scan.

The O(1) extra-space optimization was intentionally deferred as DL-001 and is not treated as a failure.

notes.note: The learner should no longer jump directly from intuition to coding. Before every coding exercise, the mentor should require completion of the Implementation Blueprint covering state, initialization, loop boundaries, previous state, global answer, and return value. Only then should coding begin.

The learner resolved Gas Station by redefining tankBalance as candidate-relative state rather than global fuel. Future greedy sessions should ask the learner to separate observation pointer, hypothesis pointer, local state, and global feasibility before coding.

Future OBS-007 revision should verify: tankBalance meaning, reset reason, why the loop does not restart, why startCandidate is only a hypothesis, why failed candidates are discarded, and why global totals are maintained separately.

The learner independently derived the invariant rather than recalling a memorized solution, proved why only the minimum previous price matters, and correctly reasoned about update order. They understood that both update orders are correct, while using past state before updating it communicates cleaner engineering intent. Reuse the abstraction `Maintain past state -> Use past state -> Update state` in Gas Station, Kadane's Algorithm, Jump Game, Prefix Sum, and HashMap prefix-state problems.

The learner reconstructed the candidate-elimination proof rather than treating it as a heuristic, kept candidate-relative running balance separate from global feasibility, and derived the implementation state before coding. Future sessions should preserve this reconstruction-first approach and explicitly distinguish intermediate state from the returned answer.

The learner correctly used the changed transaction constraint to decide that historical minPrice state is unnecessary. They separated accumulated profit from decision state, justified the prices[i + 1] loop boundary, and explained why summing every positive adjacent difference is optimal.

R2 active recall confirmed long-term strengthening for Maximum Product Subarray. The learner independently recalled why both runningMax and runningMin are needed, handled negative sign flips and zeros, preserved previous runningMax before updating runningMin, wrote the implementation from memory, dry-ran it successfully, and recalled O(n) time with O(1) space. No hints, mistakes, or weaknesses were observed.

notes.text: OBS-005 R1: derived the backward-greedy reachability algorithm from reasoning, not memory. Invariant: leftMostGoodIndex is always a good index that can reach the destination; it never moves right, so good indices form one continuous suffix and a single state variable suffices. Initialization at the last index is essential because it is the first guaranteed good index.
Greedy State Compression Principle: when all valid states form one continuous reachable region, it is sufficient to store only the boundary representative instead of every valid state.
Backward propagation is often possible when the destination is already known to be a valid state.

Misconception raised and self-corrected during OBS-005 R1: assumed the same transition stays valid if jumps must be EXACT rather than AT MOST; corrected to the condition becoming equality instead of >=. Also reasoned why left-to-right traversal cannot use this single-state greedy: there is no initially known good index to propagate from. Fully resolved, no residual confusion.

OBS-006 R1: rebuilt the interval-expansion greedy from memory and wrote the full Java implementation. Explained initialization, why the loop ends at n-2, why currentRegionEnd is updated from farthestReachableIndex, and the update order.

Region expansion and jump commitment are two separate events. farthestReachableIndex is evidence gathered while scanning the current region; the region is only fully explored when the scan reaches currentRegionEnd, and only then does the next region begin.

Misconception corrected during OBS-006 R1: used currentIndex == farthestReachableIndex as the jump-commit condition instead of currentIndex == currentRegionEnd. Corrected after Socratic probing (hint level 1, nothing revealed); the learner then explained why the current region boundary, not the newly discovered frontier, decides when a jump is committed.

OBS-008 R1: reconstructed the two-pass Candy Distribution solution from memory, O(n) time and O(n) extra space, with no hints. Retained why two directional scans are required, why one alone is insufficient, why left and right constraints are tracked separately, and why the answer is the maximum of both directional requirements.

OBS-008 R1 independence: an accumulation mistake in the first implementation was found and corrected by the learner without requesting a hint, on their own review. Genuine recall rather than guided reconstruction.

deferred_learnings.description: The standard O(n) extra-space two-pass solution is fully understood and implemented. The O(1) extra-space optimization for Candy was intentionally deferred. The learner is aware that the optimization exists but has not yet derived or internalized its construction or correctness. This should not affect completion of the current revision and should not be treated as a failure. Revisit naturally when an appropriate future problem or optimization-focused revision provides the necessary context.
Continue building intuition for recognizing HashMap pattern triggers across new problems.
deferred_learnings.evidence: CPX-002 resolved the HashMap/HashSet pattern-recognition deferred learning by deriving the repeated query, required state, minimal HashSet structure, contains-before-add invariant, and O(n)/O(n) complexity without memorized pattern recall.

OBS-001.core_mental_model: Kadane's algorithm tracks the maximum-sum non-empty subarray ending at the current index and discards a previous prefix only when that prefix is negative.
OBS-001.primary_invariant: runningSum always represents a real, non-empty subarray ending at the current index.
OBS-001.major_breakthrough: Restart decisions depend on the previous running sum: if runningSum < 0, then FutureSum > FutureSum + runningSum.
OBS-001.implementation_lesson: Initialization values must themselves satisfy the algorithm invariant. Initializing with `0` breaks the invariant because `0` may not correspond to any valid non-empty subarray. Initializing with the first element guarantees every tracked value represents a real subarray throughout execution.
OBS-001.edge_case_learned: All-negative arrays expose invalid neutral initialization for non-empty subarray problems.
OBS-001.interview_takeaway: Before using a neutral initial value, verify that it is a valid candidate under the problem constraints and loop invariant.

OBS-003.core_mental_model: For each current price, only the smallest price in the already processed prefix can maximize a one-transaction profit. Maintain that past summary, use it to evaluate the current price, then update it for future prices.
OBS-003.primary_invariant: Before processing prices[i], minPrice is the minimum value in prices[0..i-1]. The current index consumes that past state before minPrice is prepared for later indices.
OBS-003.major_breakthrough: The learner independently derived the greedy invariant and proved that any larger previous price is dominated by minPrice for every future sale price.
OBS-003.implementation_lesson: Initialize maxProfit to 0 and minPrice to prices[0]. Starting at i = 1 avoids reprocessing the initialized value; starting at i = 0 is also correct because the self-profit is 0. Computing profit before updating minPrice makes the past-state invariant and engineering intent explicit, though updating minPrice first is also correct because prices[i] - prices[i] = 0 cannot reduce maxProfit.
OBS-003.edge_case_learned: Empty input is outside the LeetCode constraint; a single price correctly returns 0, and the initialized first price remains a valid prefix minimum.
OBS-003.interview_takeaway: Use the reusable linear-scan pattern: Maintain past state -> Use past state -> Update state. Distinguish mathematical correctness from the update order that most clearly communicates the invariant.

OBS-004.core_mental_model: With unlimited transactions, profit is an accumulated answer rather than decision state. Capture every positive adjacent price increase; each downward movement ends the current transaction before the next rise begins.
OBS-004.primary_invariant: After processing pair (i, i + 1), maxProfit equals the sum of all positive adjacent differences seen so far. No historical minimum is needed because future decisions do not depend on a single earlier purchase price.
OBS-004.major_breakthrough: The learner used the changed transaction constraint to determine which historical state is no longer required, instead of carrying minPrice from Stock I by habit.
OBS-004.implementation_lesson: Start at index 0 and continue while i < prices.length - 1 because each iteration reads prices[i + 1]. These natural loop boundaries safely handle empty and single-element arrays without special-case branches.
OBS-004.edge_case_learned: An empty or one-element array executes no pair comparison and returns the initial accumulated profit of 0.
OBS-004.interview_takeaway: When constraints change, re-derive the decision state. Maintain state only when future decisions need it; keep the accumulated answer separate from that state.

OBS-006.core_mental_model: A greedy solution does not always choose the next action. Sometimes it repeatedly expands the reachable frontier.
OBS-006.primary_invariant: currentRegionEnd represents the boundary of the currently explorable region, while farthestReachableIndex represents the boundary discovered for the next explorable region.
OBS-006.major_breakthrough: Stopped thinking in terms of jumping from index to index and instead reasoned in terms of expanding consecutive reachable regions.
OBS-006.implementation_lesson: The loop iterates over launch positions, not landing positions. The last index is a destination, so it is intentionally excluded from exploration.
OBS-006.edge_case_learned: Initialization and loop boundaries are coupled. Changing one requires re-establishing the algorithm's invariant and validating all edge cases.
OBS-006.interview_takeaway: When a greedy solution feels unintuitive, first identify what state is expanding. In Jump Game II, the expanding state is not the current index-it is the reachable frontier.

OBS-002.core_mental_model: Maximum Product Subarray requires tracking both runningMax and runningMin because multiplying by a negative value can swap the best and worst product states.
OBS-002.primary_invariant: After processing index i, runningMax is the maximum product of a non-empty subarray ending at i and runningMin is the minimum product of a non-empty subarray ending at i.
OBS-002.major_breakthrough: R2 active recall showed the problem has transitioned from understanding toward long-term memory: the learner reproduced the intuition, invariant, transitions, implementation, dry run, and complexity from memory without hints.
OBS-002.implementation_lesson: Implementation details now follow from the state definition: initialize with nums[0], start after the initialized index, preserve prevMax and prevMin before mutation, update globalMax immediately after computing runningMax, and return globalMax rather than local DP state.
OBS-002.edge_case_learned: Negative values and single-element arrays expose invalid initialization and missing global-answer maintenance.
OBS-002.interview_takeaway: For product-state DP, explicitly explain why both extremes are carried, how negative sign flips swap roles, how zero permits restart through the current element, and why previous runningMax must be saved before updating runningMin.

OBS-007.core_mental_model: Gas Station is a candidate-elimination scan: startCandidate is the current hypothesis, tankBalance belongs only to that candidate, and global feasibility is a separate condition.
OBS-007.primary_invariant: Before the first negative tankBalance from candidate S through index i, every prefix from S is non-negative. When balance(S, i) becomes negative, balance(k, i) is negative for every k in [S, i], so each is eliminated.
OBS-007.major_breakthrough: The learner reconstructed the mathematical candidate-elimination proof from first principles and independently derived the required implementation state.
OBS-007.implementation_lesson: The scan pointer keeps moving forward when the candidate changes; resetting tankBalance starts a new candidate-relative state. startCandidate can temporarily equal n after the final transition, but total feasibility guarantees that value is not returned. Separate total available and total consumed are equivalent to one totalBalance accumulator.
OBS-007.edge_case_learned: Local tankBalance evaluates one candidate, while totalGas >= totalCost determines whether any full traversal exists. The final candidate transition may create an intermediate sentinel state without creating an invalid return value.
OBS-007.interview_takeaway: Reconstruct the proof, derive the state from it, then implement. Keep local candidate feasibility separate from global feasibility and explain why eliminated candidates are mathematically dominated.

OBS-008.core_mental_model: Candy is a bidirectional constraint-satisfaction problem: one pass can satisfy only one adjacent direction, so the standard solution computes both directional requirements and keeps the maximum at every index.
OBS-008.primary_invariant: After the left-to-right pass, every child with a higher rating than the left neighbor has more candies than that left neighbor. After the right-to-left pass and max merge, every child also satisfies the right-neighbor constraint without breaking the left-neighbor constraint.
OBS-008.major_breakthrough: The learner realized that local greedy decisions from one direction are insufficient because the opposite adjacent constraint is independent and can force a larger assignment.
OBS-008.implementation_lesson: Each directional pass owns one constraint. The final answer is the sum of max(leftRequirement[i], rightRequirement[i]) for each child in the standard O(n) extra-space implementation.
OBS-008.edge_case_learned: Peaks, valleys, equal ratings, increasing runs, and decreasing runs expose why both directional constraints must be evaluated independently.
OBS-008.interview_takeaway: Explain the standard O(n)/O(n) solution as two independent constraint passes plus a max merge. Mention that O(1) space optimization exists but was intentionally deferred and is not part of the current mastered scope.

CPX-001.core_mental_model: Repeated queries about previously processed values often indicate that a HashMap or HashSet should be considered. For Two Sum, reframe pair-search into missing-partner search: requiredPartner = target - currentElement.
CPX-001.primary_invariant: Before processing index i, the HashMap contains every value encountered from indices 0..i-1 mapped to its index. Therefore, if the required partner already exists in the processed portion, it can be found in average O(1).
CPX-001.major_breakthrough: The learner stopped thinking about repeatedly searching for a pair and instead identified the repeated query: Have I already seen the required partner? This directly determined the needed state and data structure.
CPX-001.implementation_lesson: Choose the HashMap key based on the repeated query. Since the repeated query is whether a value has already been seen, store value -> index. Search before insertion so the current element never matches itself.
CPX-001.edge_case_learned: Search before insertion prevents the current element from matching itself when target equals currentElement * 2.
CPX-001.interview_takeaway: Explain O(n) complexity by separating n iterations from O(1) average HashMap operations per iteration.

CPX-002.core_mental_model: For Contains Duplicate, the repeated query is: Have I already seen this value? Because only membership is needed, the required state is the set of previously processed values and the minimal data structure is a HashSet.
CPX-002.primary_invariant: Immediately before processing index i, the HashSet contains exactly the distinct values encountered in indices 0..i-1.
CPX-002.major_breakthrough: The learner derived the solution from first principles by moving from brute-force search to repeated-query identification, then from required state to the minimal data structure.
CPX-002.implementation_lesson: Check set.contains(nums[i]) before set.add(nums[i]) so the maintained state represents only previously processed elements and the current element cannot match itself.
CPX-002.edge_case_learned: Empty and single-element arrays need no special branches: the loop behavior and final false return already satisfy the problem constraints.
CPX-002.interview_takeaway: Explain correctness by noting that every duplicate has a second occurrence; when the second occurrence is reached, the first occurrence is already stored. Explain O(n) as one traversal with average O(1) HashSet contains/add operations and O(n) worst-case space.

OBS-002.text: Incorrect initialization using Integer.MIN_VALUE / Integer.MAX_VALUE instead of values that satisfy the state invariant.
Forgot to use preserved prevMax / prevMin values after storing them.
Returned local runningMax instead of maintaining and returning a global answer.
Placed globalMax update outside the loop instead of immediately after computing each new runningMax.
Minor syntax omissions during code recall.
Resolved in R2: no initialization, previous-state, global-answer, update-ordering, syntax, dry-run, or complexity weakness appeared during the 2026-07-20 active recall.

OBS-003.text: lack of proof for greedy correctness.
confusion regarding why larger previous values can be safely discarded.

OBS-004.text: carrying unnecessary historical state from the single-transaction variant.
conflating accumulated profit with algorithm decision state.
relying on memorized loop boundaries instead of deriving them from prices[i + 1].
lacking a clear optimality explanation for the positive-difference greedy rule.

OBS-006.text: Initially attempted to understand the algorithm through concrete jump paths rather than reachable regions.
Required formal justification for every variable before internalizing the greedy invariant.
Initially proposed alternative initialization without considering loop invariant preservation.

OBS-007.text: initially treated tankBalance as global fuel instead of candidate-relative fuel.
initially expected the loop to restart after a failed candidate.
initially tied candidate replacement to changing the scan pointer.
needed to separate local tankBalance reasoning from global total feasibility.

personal_playbook: When solving greedy problems, first identify what state grows after every decision. If that state is a frontier or region rather than an individual position, reason about expanding boundaries instead of selecting explicit paths.
Before coding any understood algorithm, complete the Implementation Blueprint: state, initialization, loop boundaries, previous-state needs, global answer ownership, and return value.

Greedy hypothesis scan pattern: identify the observation pointer, identify the hypothesis pointer, define the maintained state, define the invariant, define the failure condition, decide when the hypothesis changes, decide whether the maintained state changes meaning, reset state only when its semantic meaning changes, and separate local reasoning from global feasibility.
State variables should be named and initialized from their semantic responsibility before writing update equations.

For a linear scan, state exactly what information from the processed prefix future decisions need. Maintain that past summary, use it against the current item, then update it for the future. Prove why discarded past values are dominated.

For candidate-elimination scans, prove the failed segment impossible before advancing the candidate. Keep candidate-relative state separate from global feasibility, and derive implementation variables from those proof obligations.
When a constraint changes, re-derive whether historical decision state is still needed. Keep an accumulated answer separate from decision state, and derive loop bounds from every indexed access.
For bidirectional adjacent constraints, assign each pass one direction of responsibility, then merge requirements with max so satisfying the second direction does not break the first.

For lookup problems, identify the repeated query first, then derive the required state and choose the smallest data structure that answers that query instantly. Search before insertion when the current item must not be allowed to match itself.
When the repeated query only asks whether a value exists, store membership in a HashSet; use a HashMap only when the answer also needs metadata such as an index, count, or group.

strengths: Observation pointer and hypothesis pointer separation
Scan pointer vs hypothesis pointer separation
HashMap state selection from repeated query
HashSet state selection from membership-only repeated query
General algorithm covers empty and single-element arrays without special branches
common_errors: Mixed local DP state with final answer
improvement_notes: The learner consistently derives correct algorithms but occasionally loses marks while translating the algorithm into code. Future sessions should require an Implementation Blueprint before coding.
For greedy scans, define whether each variable is local to the current hypothesis or global to feasibility before coding.
For greedy problems, resetting a variable must mean its semantic owner changed, not simply that the code pattern says reset.
Keep local hypothesis state separate from global feasibility state in the Implementation Blueprint.
OBS-003 revision confirmed that the learner can justify initialization, loop boundaries, update order, and the dominance proof for a past-state greedy scan without hints.
OBS-007 R1 confirmed correct reconstruction, state derivation, implementation from memory, and dry-run validation for the candidate-elimination scan.
OBS-004 R1 confirmed that the learner can derive state necessity from constraints, justify adjacency-based greedy accumulation, and implement safe loop bounds from the indexed access.

OBS-002 R2 confirmed that the learner can reproduce product-DP state definitions, previous-state preservation, global answer maintenance, dry run, and O(n)/O(1) implementation from memory without hints.

OBS-008 confirmed that the learner can derive two directional passes from independent adjacent constraints and explain why max-merging the two directional assignments is the minimum valid distribution.
CPX-001 confirmed the learner can derive value-to-index HashMap state from the repeated query: Have I already seen this required partner?
CPX-002 reinforced that implementation order must preserve the state definition: before index i, the HashSet contains exactly values from indices 0..i-1.
